findings.title: Swap invariant is weakened by a 100x scaling mismatch, allowing near-total reserve drains

best_agent_match.title: Swap invariant is weakened by a 100x scaling mismatch, allowing near-total reserve drains

findings.title: Referral fee transfers are excluded from reserve accounting, corrupting reserves after swaps

best_agent_match.title: Referral fee transfers are excluded from reserve accounting, corrupting reserves after every swap

findings.title: Referral rewards are attributed to a caller-controlled `to` address, enabling likely self-referral farming

best_agent_match.title: Referral rewards are attributed to the user-controlled `to` address, enabling likely self-referral farming

rejected_candidates.title: Reentrancy via callback before K invariant validation
rejected_candidates.reason: `swap()` is protected by the `lock` modifier, so reentrant calls into the pair's state-changing entrypoints revert. Calling `NimbusCall` before the invariant check is the standard flash-swap pattern and is not by itself an exploitable reentrancy bug here.

rejected_candidates.title: Unvalidated referral program can steal user funds
rejected_candidates.reason: The referral program address is a trusted factory-controlled dependency, not a permissionless attack surface. Also, receiving ERC20 transfers does not invoke a recipient fallback, so the stated fallback-based theft path is incorrect.

rejected_candidates.title: Arbitrary external call enables flash loan attacks
rejected_candidates.reason: Allowing a callback to `to` is the normal flash-swap mechanism for Uniswap V2-style pairs, not a standalone vulnerability. Profitability still depends on external market conditions, not a flaw in this contract.

rejected_candidates.title: Referral fees transferred before K validation
rejected_candidates.reason: If the later `require` fails, the whole transaction reverts, including the ERC20 transfers and `recordFee()` call. The fees are not permanently lost on a reverted swap.

rejected_candidates.title: No slippage protection allows unlimited slippage
rejected_candidates.reason: This pair is a low-level AMM primitive. Slippage protection is normally enforced by routers or callers, so the absence of min-out parameters in the pair itself is not reportable.

rejected_candidates.title: Permissionless skim and sync functions
rejected_candidates.reason: These functions are standard for Uniswap V2-style pairs. `skim()` only transfers excess balances above reserves, and `sync()` only aligns reserves to actual balances; neither creates a realistic standalone exploit here.

rejected_candidates.title: Unusual referral fee denominator
rejected_candidates.reason: Using `1994` is unusual but plausibly intentional to approximate a 0.15% referral charge. On its own this is not evidence of an exploitable bug or meaningful protocol harm.
